text:
This book teaches tritone substitution as a practical soloing tool for jazz guitar, structured around a single canonical progression—the II V I realized as Dm7 | Db7 | CM7—and built outward from that foundation into a reusable, transposable framework. The pedagogical arc moves from concept to application: the master first establishes the substitution principle (replacing a V7 chord with a dom7 chord a tritone away), then systematically maps every available soloing option over the substitute chord back to its harmonic function on the original dominant.

Chapter 1 introduces the book's core analytical method: four soloing options over the substituted dominant—dom7 arpeggio, dom9 arpeggio, major pentatonic scale, and Mixolydian mode—each subjected to tone-mapping analysis that identifies exactly which intervals of the substitute chord correspond to which altered extensions (b9, #11, b13) of the original V7. This interval-mapping procedure is the master's characteristic move: every lick is deconstructed measure-by-measure and every scale degree is assigned a harmonic label, so the student understands not just what to play but why it works.

The book's terminology is precise and load-bearing. Tritone substitution, the four soloing options, the altered tones (b9, #11, b13), approach notes, and the Dorian bebop scale (defined as the Dorian mode with an added chromatic passing tone) are all introduced as reusable technical vocabulary. These terms are not decorative—they anchor the tone-mapping analysis and make the framework portable across keys.

The master's prescribed method is explicitly analytical rather than intuitive: learn the substitution principle, identify the substitute chord, apply one of the four soloing options, trace each tone back to its altered-extension function on the original dominant, and then transpose the entire framework to new keys. The Dorian bebop scale receives specific attention as a soloing tool over minor chords in the II V I context, extending the harmonic vocabulary beyond the dominant substitution itself. The overall pedagogical stance is that understanding the intervallic logic of substitution—not merely memorizing licks—is what allows the student to generate material independently in any key.
